learning_objectives: Identify database security threats and attack vectors
Apply the principle of least privilege to database accounts

lecture_notes.introduction: Databases contain an organization's most valuable asset: its data. Database security ensures that data confidentiality, integrity, and availability are maintained against both external attacks and insider threats.
key_concepts: Database threat landscape: SQL injection, privilege escalation, data exfiltration
Access control: GRANT/REVOKE, role-based access, row-level security
Authentication: database accounts vs integrated authentication
Least privilege: application accounts should have minimum required permissions
Database activity monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring PostgreSQL roles with least-privilege access for an application: separate read-only, read-write, and admin roles

review.value: Application database accounts should have only SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE on specific tables they need. Never use sa/root/postgres for application connections. Create separate accounts for each application tier.
PostgreSQL RLS enables policies that filter rows based on the current user. Example: a multi-tenant application where each tenant can only see their own data, enforced at the database level.

learning_objectives: Exploit SQL injection vulnerabilities in a lab environment
Implement parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
Configure WAF rules for SQL injection detection
readings: The Web Application Hacker's Handbook SQL Injection chapter
OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet

lecture_notes.introduction: SQL injection remains one of the most critical web application vulnerabilities. Understanding both the attack and defense is essential for securing database-backed applications.
key_concepts: SQL injection types: UNION-based, blind (boolean/time), error-based, out-of-band
Parameterized queries (prepared statements) as the primary defense
Stored procedures: security benefits and limitations
ORM security: does the ORM prevent injection?
WAF rules for SQL injection detection and limitations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploiting SQL injection in DVWA to extract the entire database, then fixing the vulnerability with parameterized queries and demonstrating the fix

review.value: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', (user_id,)). The database engine treats the parameter as data, never as SQL code. This is the only reliable prevention.
ORMs like SQLAlchemy generally prevent injection for standard queries. Raw SQL in ORMs (session.execute(text(user_input))) reintroduces the vulnerability. Always parameterize even in ORMs.

learning_objectives: Implement Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) for data at rest
Design column-level encryption for sensitive fields
Apply dynamic data masking for non-production environments

lecture_notes.introduction: Encryption protects database contents even when access controls fail. Data masking ensures sensitive data is not exposed in development, testing, and analytics environments.
key_concepts: Transparent Data Encryption: encrypts files on disk, transparent to applications
Column-level encryption: encrypting specific sensitive columns with pgcrypto
Application-level encryption: encrypt before storing, decrypt after retrieval
Key management for database encryption
Dynamic data masking: on-the-fly obfuscation for non-privileged users
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing column-level encryption with pgcrypto for SSN and credit card fields, and configuring dynamic data masking for a reporting role

review.value: TDE protects against physical media theft (stolen disk). Column encryption protects against unauthorized database access (compromised account). TDE is transparent; column encryption requires application changes.
Encryption is only as strong as key management. Store encryption keys in a KMS, not in the database or application code. Implement key rotation without re-encrypting all data (envelope encryption).

lecture_notes.introduction: Database auditing provides visibility into who accessed what data, when, and how. This is essential for security monitoring, compliance, and forensic investigation.
key_concepts: Audit logging: DDL, DML, DCL events
pgAudit extension for granular PostgreSQL auditing
Database Activity Monitoring: inline vs out-of-band
Anomaly detection: unusual query patterns, bulk data access
Compliance requirements: PCI-DSS, HIPAA audit mandates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring pgAudit to log all data access, setting up alerts for bulk SELECT statements, and demonstrating how audit logs can trace a data exfiltration event

review.value: Extension that provides detailed audit logging including the SQL statement, user, timestamp, and affected objects. Configure per-role and per-object auditing for targeted monitoring without performance overhead.
Alert on queries returning unusually large result sets, SELECT * on sensitive tables, or new SQL patterns from application accounts. These may indicate SQL injection or insider data theft.

class_schedule.topic: Database Backup Security and Disaster Recovery

lecture_notes.introduction: Backups are both a security control and a security risk. They protect against data loss but are also valuable targets for attackers seeking to steal data.
key_concepts: Backup types: full, incremental, WAL archiving for PITR
Backup encryption: encrypting backup files at rest
Backup integrity: hash verification, test restores
Backup access control: who can access backup files?
Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) for ransomware response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring encrypted PostgreSQL backups with pg_dump, verifying integrity, and performing a point-in-time recovery to a state before a simulated ransomware encryption

review.value: Backups contain all database data. An unencrypted backup stolen from a file share is a full data breach. Encrypt all backups, restrict access to backup storage, and monitor for unauthorized access.
Point-in-Time Recovery using WAL archives allows restoration to any second in time. Critical for ransomware response: restore to the moment before encryption began.

class_schedule.topic: NoSQL Security and Cloud Database Security
learning_objectives: Identify security challenges unique to NoSQL databases
Implement security for MongoDB, Redis, and Elasticsearch
readings: AWS RDS and DynamoDB security documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: NoSQL databases and cloud-managed services have different security models than traditional RDBMS. Many high-profile breaches have involved misconfigured MongoDB and Elasticsearch instances.
key_concepts: MongoDB security: authentication, authorization, encryption, network binding
Redis security: ACLs, protected mode, TLS
Elasticsearch security: X-Pack, role-based access, field-level security
Cloud database security: VPC placement, IAM authentication, encryption at rest
NoSQL injection attacks and prevention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Finding and securing an intentionally misconfigured MongoDB instance (no auth, bound to 0.0.0.0), then configuring authentication, TLS, and network restrictions

review.value: MongoDB historically shipped with no authentication and bound to all interfaces. Thousands of databases were ransomed (MongoLock). Always enable authentication, bind to localhost or private IP, and require TLS.

Place cloud databases in private subnets with no public IP. Use IAM authentication instead of database passwords where available. Enable encryption at rest (default on most managed services) and in transit (require TLS).

quizzes.topic: Database Security, SQL Injection, and Encryption

questions.question: A developer writes the following Python code to query a database: query = f"SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = '{request.args.get('id')}'" cursor.execute(query). Explain the vulnerability, provide a specific exploit payload, and rewrite the code securely.
questions.answer: Vulnerability: String interpolation (f-string) concatenates user input directly into the SQL query, enabling SQL injection. Exploit payload: id=' OR '1'='1 produces: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = '' OR '1'='1' which returns all orders for all customers. More dangerous: id='; DROP TABLE orders;-- Secure rewrite: query = 'SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = %s' cursor.execute(query, (request.args.get('id'),)). The %s placeholder is parameterized; psycopg2 handles escaping and type conversion, ensuring the input is treated as data.

questions.question: A healthcare application stores patient SSNs encrypted with AES-256 using pgcrypto. The encryption key is stored in the same database in a 'config' table readable by the application account. Explain why this is insecure and design a proper key management approach.
questions.answer: The key stored in the same database defeats encryption: anyone who compromises the database (SQL injection, stolen backup, DBA access) gets both the encrypted data and the key to decrypt it. Proper approach: (1) Store the encryption key in an external KMS (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault) that the application accesses via API. (2) Use envelope encryption: the KMS holds the master key, which encrypts/decrypts data encryption keys (DEKs). The DEK is stored encrypted alongside the data. (3) The application authenticates to the KMS with IAM role or certificate, not a password in the database. (4) Implement key rotation: generate a new DEK periodically, re-encrypt data. The master key in KMS can rotate transparently. (5) Separate access: database compromise alone does not yield the master key.

rubric.key: Explains why co-located key is insecure

questions.question: Your organization's pgAudit logs reveal that the application service account executed: SELECT * FROM patients WHERE diagnosis LIKE '%HIV%' at 3:00 AM. The application does not have a feature that queries by diagnosis. What does this indicate and what should you investigate?
questions.answer: This indicates unauthorized data access, likely through: (1) SQL injection in the application allowing an attacker to run arbitrary queries through the application's database connection, or (2) Compromised application credentials being used directly against the database. Investigation steps: (1) Check application server logs for the corresponding HTTP request at 3:00 AM to identify the source (SQL injection vector or direct database connection). (2) If an HTTP request exists, analyze the web server access logs for the source IP and the request parameters that triggered this query. (3) If no HTTP request exists, the credentials are being used directly: check database connection logs for the source IP. (4) Assess scope: run a full audit log analysis for this time window to identify all queries run. (5) Containment: rotate the application database credentials immediately. (6) Determine if patient data was exfiltrated; this is a HIPAA breach requiring notification if PHI was accessed by an unauthorized party.

questions.question: A company's Elasticsearch cluster was found exposed to the internet with no authentication, containing 50 million customer records. Describe three specific misconfigurations that allowed this and the controls that would prevent each.
questions.answer: Misconfiguration 1: Elasticsearch bound to 0.0.0.0 (network.host setting) instead of localhost or a private IP. Prevention: bind to 127.0.0.1 or the private network interface. Use a reverse proxy for any external access. Misconfiguration 2: No authentication enabled. Elasticsearch historically shipped with X-Pack security disabled by default. Prevention: enable X-Pack security, create users with role-based access control, require API keys or username/password for all access. Misconfiguration 3: No firewall rules restricting access to port 9200. The cloud security group or iptables rules allowed 0.0.0.0/0 on port 9200. Prevention: restrict security group to only application server IPs. Add Elasticsearch to a private subnet with no internet gateway. Additional: enable TLS for all Elasticsearch communication and implement audit logging.

major_assignments.title: Database Security Hardening and Assessment
major_assignments.description: Harden a provided database installation from default to secure configuration, perform a security assessment, and produce a hardening guide.
objectives: Harden PostgreSQL from default installation to CIS Benchmark compliance
Implement encryption, auditing, and access controls
Perform a security assessment of the hardened database
deliverables: Security assessment report (before and after hardening)

criteria: Encryption at rest and in transit enabled
Comprehensive database hardening from default to secure state
Thorough security assessment with before/after comparison
Professional hardening guide suitable for production use

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of database security concepts and SQL knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on database security configuration and testing exercises

major_assignments.description: Database hardening and security assessment project

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering SQL injection, access control, and encryption

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all database security topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All reports and scripts must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: SQL injection testing must only be performed against designated lab databases. Accessing other students' databases is prohibited.
course_policies.lab_safety: Database modifications must only be made to your assigned lab instances. Modifying shared infrastructure is grounds for course failure.
